title: Combining Multiple Ranked Lists
concept: The general problem of merging rankings from different retrievers (dense, sparse, multi-query) into one order.

exercises.prompt: Why do we use both keyword (sparse) and semantic (dense) search in modern retrieval systems?

options.text: Keyword search is superior for all types of search queries.
They capture different types of relevance (exact matches vs. conceptual meaning).
They use the exact same algorithm under the hood.

exercises.explain: In previous lessons on hybrid search, we saw that keyword search excels at exact terms (like product IDs) while vector search excels at capturing conceptual meaning.

cards.title: The Ranking Clash
cards.body: In search systems, we often use different tools to find documents: keyword matching finds exact words, while vector search finds matching concepts. But when they return different lists, how do we decide which document actually comes first?

cards.title: What is Rank Fusion?
cards.body: Rank fusion is the process of combining multiple ranked lists of documents—often generated by different search algorithms—into a single, consolidated ranking. This allows systems to leverage the unique strengths of both keyword matching and conceptual understanding.

cards.title: The Two Critics Analogy
cards.body: Imagine you are hiring a chef and ask two food critics for their top recommendations. One critic loves comfort food (like keyword search) and the other loves artistic presentation (like semantic search); you need a fair way to combine their ranked lists to find the absolute best candidate.

cards.title: Blending the Lists
cards.body: If a document is ranked #1 by a keyword search but #50 by a semantic search, rank fusion blends these two positions. Instead of picking one retriever over the other, the system calculates a combined rank where this document still ends up near the top.

cards.title: Why Not Just Average the Scores?
cards.body: It seems easy to just average the search scores, but this is a trap because different systems use completely different scales (like 0 to 1 vs. 0 to 100+). Rank fusion solves this by focusing on the relative order (the rank) rather than the raw scores.

cards.title: Scores vs. Ranks
cards.body: Compare how raw score merging fails due to incompatible scales, whereas rank-based merging creates a fair, balanced output.

right.text: Combining Rank #1 and Rank #2 creates a balanced order.

exercises.prompt: Why can't we simply add the raw scores from a keyword retriever and a semantic retriever together?

options.text: Because semantic retrievers are always faster than keyword retrievers.
Because different retrievers output scores on completely different scales.
Because databases can only store one type of ranking at a time.
Because users prefer raw numbers over visual ordered lists.

exercises.explain: Different retrievers use different scales (e.g., BM25 scores can go above 30, while cosine similarity is capped at 1.0). Comparing these raw scores directly is like comparing apples to oranges.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Combining keyword search and semantic search generally leads to better retrieval results than using either one alone.
exercises.explain: By combining both dense (semantic) and sparse (keyword) retrievers, you get the best of both worlds: exact word matching and conceptual understanding.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing term that describes combining multiple search lists into one.
exercises.sentence: We use rank fusion to merge different retrieval results into a single ordered list.
exercises.explain: Rank fusion takes multiple ordered lists (like those from keyword and semantic search) and merges them into one single ranked list.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following scenarios describes an AI application performing rank fusion?

options.text: An AI system merges a keyword-search list and a vector-search list into a single top-10 list for a user query.
An AI system converts a collection of text documents into 1536-dimensional embeddings.
An AI system partitions a database index to make vector searches run faster.

exercises.explain: Option A describes the core challenge of rank fusion: taking multiple lists sorted by different criteria and merging them into one final list. Options B and C describe vector generation and indexing, not ranking fusion.

exercises.prompt: Imagine you have a dense retriever that outputs cosine similarity scores (from 0 to 1) and a BM25 retriever that outputs raw matching scores (from 0 to 30+). How can you fairly combine these lists to get a single, unified ranking?

options.text: By averaging the raw similarity scores directly across all retrievers.
By converting scores into ranks first, then combining those ranks.
By ignoring the sparse retriever completely because dense is always better.
By sorting documents alphabetically to resolve any ordering conflicts.

exercises.explain: Rank fusion is necessary because different retrieval methods (like keyword-based and concept-based search) use completely different scoring scales. By converting these raw scores into simple rank positions first, we can combine their results fairly and find the best documents.
